insight: In base set only, P2 likely has the winning advantage, and the natural animus build (DD|DD|DDA|TTD) for P1 gives the closest games but still tends to lose. Testing by multiple experts confirmed that T3 animus for P2 loses to natural animus, and third engineer for P1 only achieves a 50% win rate (equivalent to P1 winning coin flip) against a P2 who also goes third engineer.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion among top players about base set opening theory and P1 vs P2 advantage.

insight: In Thunderhead sets, threatening Thunderhead (or actually buying it) is considered a strong line, and DDC (double drone, conduit) opening is assessed as winning in far too many Thunderhead games, even accounting for the presence of Vivid Drone and Galvani Drone which introduce some uncertainty.
author: punf
context: mqp and punf discussing optimal lines in a Thunderhead set after playing multiple games.

insight: When facing Tarsier spam (fast animus into two Tarsiers per turn), Rhino spam is a strong counter because it prevents the opponent from droning and mirroring Tarsiers for free; steel0229e won three consecutive games against this strategy using Rhino spam.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion about countering fast Tarsier openings, prompted by a player struggling to beat the strategy.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer is significantly slower than Savior as a defensive engine: Savior activates in roughly 4 turns and then provides 4 HP per turn of soak, whereas Zemora requires a Conduit, another Conduit, then Zemora itself, then 6 turns of charging before it contributes meaningfully, making it much harder to race against fast Tarsier pressure.
author: crash_overlord
context: Comparison of Zemora and Savior defensive timelines during a discussion about alternative starting positions and Zemora viability.

insight: In a set with Vivid Drone and Zemora Voidbringer, opening T1 Conduit (1C) as P1 can be correct and winning, as crash_overlord tested and found that Zemora with Vivid beats Tarsier-focused P2 responses including Blastforge lines and Rhino rushes, even without Synthesis support.
author: crash_overlord
context: Live testing of unconventional T1 Conduit opening in Vivid+Zemora sets during a discussion about alternative starting positions.

insight: Redeemer is generally strongest when: (1) there is cheap soak available for you to absorb its charges (e.g. Plexo Cell in the same color), and (2) your opponent lacks efficient counter-damage to punish the charges coming back. It is weaker in sets with fast red units or strong attack-cost units like Bloodrager, which can make denying absorb difficult.
author: wilson0862
context: Community discussion explaining when Redeemer is the correct buy, prompted by new players struggling to identify Redeemer sets.

insight: Redeemer is a strong counter to Wild Drone strategies because the threat of Redeemer charges makes Wild Drones inefficient; a player who built up 9 Redeemer power forced their opponent's Wild Drones to die in defense.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of Redeemer matchups after a player reported losing a Wild Drone game to Redeemer.

insight: When planning to build Redeemer, the key strategic question is not just when to buy Redeemer but when to buy the Conduit that enables it; punf frames this as the central timing decision for Redeemer strategies, suggesting the Conduit buy timing is the crux of the opening.
author: punf
context: Brief expert exchange about Redeemer strategy, with punf reframing the standard question about when to buy Redeemer.

insight: Top player Weiseguy's heuristic for Redeemer: buy it when you have 8GGB (8 gold, 1 green, 1 blue income) and want more attack. This framing implies Redeemer fits into a mid-econ build that has already secured green and blue tech rather than being an early rush unit.
author: mqp
context: mqp relaying advice from a top player about when to buy Redeemer during a community discussion on the unit.

insight: The presence of even a single advanced unit can change base-set opening theory drastically; base-set theory (e.g. P1/P2 animus timing debates) is primarily of theoretical value, since real games include 8+ random units whose interactions vastly multiply complexity and alter optimal timings.
author: steel0229e
context: steel0229e summarizing why extended base-set-only theoretical debate has limited practical application to normal Prismata games.

insight: P1 and P2 drone supply was equalized to 27 vs 27 in a patch on May 6, 2017, correcting an earlier asymmetry where P1 had 26 and P2 had 27 supply drones.
author: jamesa7171
context: Correction during a discussion about P1/P2 resource asymmetry where mqp had referenced the old 27 vs 26 supply split.

insight: Rhino is generally better than Tarsier in a simple scenario where it absorbs 1 damage (e.g. on turn 3 of a basic opening), providing both offensive pressure and defensive absorption simultaneously, though Tarsier is stronger earlier in the game for raw attack output.
author: mqp
context: mqp commenting on Rhino vs Tarsier efficiency in response to a player posting opening lines with early Blastforge.
